title: Putin Likes His Worst Option in Ukraine
gist: Carl Bildt argues Putin faces a binary choice between a ceasefire and escalation in Ukraine, and that all signs point to him choosing escalation despite it being the worse option for Russia itself.

insights.claim: Facing a quagmire in eastern Ukraine and escalating Ukrainian attacks inside Russia, Vladimir Putin's realistic options have narrowed to two: seek a ceasefire, or pursue further escalation.
insights.speaker: Carl Bildt
insights.so_what: Investors tracking war-risk premia and Russia-exposed assets should treat de-escalation as the less likely path given the author's read of the signals.
insights.quote: Faced with a quagmire in eastern Ukraine and escalating Ukrainian attacks within Russia, Vladimir Putin must either seek a ceasefire or pursue some form of escalation.
insights.locator: Faced with a quagmire in eastern Ukraine

insights.claim: All available signs indicate Putin is set on pursuing escalation rather than a clearheaded ceasefire, despite escalation being objectively the worse choice given the state of the war.
insights.speaker: Carl Bildt
insights.so_what: Signals a higher probability of further military escalation in the Russia-Ukraine war rather than negotiated de-escalation in the near term.
insights.quote: If Russian President Vladimir Putin were to make a clearheaded assessment of the state of his war with Ukraine, he would seek a ceasefire. But his head apparently is not clear, because all signs point to him pursuing some kind of escalation.

insights.claim: If Putin embarks on further escalation in Ukraine, the consequences will worsen for all parties involved, but the author expects the costs to fall hardest on Putin himself.
insights.speaker: Carl Bildt
insights.so_what: Implies elevated regime/leadership risk for Putin specifically from further escalation, not just broader geopolitical risk.
insights.quote: If he does indeed embark on such a course, he will make the situation worse for everyone, but primarily for himself.
